title: Trait conformance: infrastructure
analysis:
The task requires adding a new pure, public `check_trait_conformance(structure_members, trait_def) -> Vec<ConformanceError>` function and a new `ConformanceError` enum to the reify-compiler crate.

Currently, trait conformance checking exists as a private `check_trait_conformance` function (line 4399) with a heavy signature — it takes an EntityDefRef, trait_registry, CompilationScope, mutable value_cells/constraints vecs, and diagnostics. It both validates conformance AND injects trait defaults (value cells, constraints). It emits errors as `Diagnostic` values pushed into a mutable vec.

The task asks for a new, focused public function with a clean API:
- Input: `structure_members: &HashMap<String, Type>` (member name -> type) and `trait_def: &CompiledTrait`
- Output: `Vec<ConformanceError>` — structured error types, not Diagnostics
- Scope: For each `RequirementKind::Param` in `trait_def.required_members`, verify the structure has a param with matching name and exact type (using Type PartialEq).

This is infrastructure for a larger trait system — the pure function checks one trait's requirements against a flat member map, while callers handle refinement hierarchy walking and default injection.

The existing private function must be renamed to avoid collision (it's called once at line 2685). Renaming to `check_and_apply_trait_conformance` captures its dual role (check + inject defaults).

prerequisites.description: Rename existing private `check_trait_conformance` (line 4399) to `check_and_apply_trait_conformance` and update the single call site at line 2685. This avoids name collision with the new public function. No behavior change — purely mechanical rename.
tests: cargo test --package reify-compiler (all existing tests pass after rename)

steps.description: Write failing test `conformance_empty_trait_no_errors`: construct a CompiledTrait with empty required_members and an arbitrary structure_members HashMap. Call `check_trait_conformance` and assert it returns an empty Vec. This test fails because the function and ConformanceError type don't exist yet.

Add `ConformanceError` enum with two variants: `MissingParam { name: String, expected_type: Type }` and `TypeMismatch { name: String, expected_type: Type, actual_type: Type }`. Derive Debug, Clone, PartialEq. Add `pub fn check_trait_conformance(structure_members: &HashMap<String, Type>, trait_def: &CompiledTrait) -> Vec<ConformanceError>` that returns an empty Vec. Place the type near the other trait-related types (after line ~80) and the function after `collect_all_requirements`.

Write failing test `conformance_missing_param_error`: construct a CompiledTrait with one `RequirementKind::Param(Type::Scalar { dimension: DimensionVector::LENGTH })` named 'width', and an empty structure_members. Assert the result contains exactly one `ConformanceError::MissingParam { name: "width", expected_type: LENGTH }`. Fails because function returns empty vec.

Implement missing-param detection in `check_trait_conformance`: iterate `trait_def.required_members`, for each `RequirementKind::Param(expected_type)`, if `structure_members` does not contain the requirement name, push `ConformanceError::MissingParam { name, expected_type }`.

Write failing test `conformance_type_mismatch_error`: construct a CompiledTrait requiring param 'mass' of type Mass, and structure_members containing 'mass' mapped to Length. Assert result contains exactly one `ConformanceError::TypeMismatch { name: "mass", expected_type: MASS, actual_type: LENGTH }`. Fails because the function doesn't check types yet — it sees the key exists and skips.

Add type-mismatch detection: when `structure_members` contains the requirement name but `actual_type != expected_type`, push `ConformanceError::TypeMismatch { name, expected_type, actual_type }`. The full Param branch now: lookup name -> missing -> MissingParam, found + wrong type -> TypeMismatch, found + correct type -> no error.

Write test `conformance_satisfied_param_no_errors`: construct a CompiledTrait requiring param 'width' of type Length, and structure_members containing 'width' mapped to Length. Assert result is empty. This should pass immediately (green) — validates the happy path.

Write test `conformance_multiple_requirements_mixed`: construct a CompiledTrait with three Param requirements: 'width' (Length), 'mass' (Mass), 'name' (String). Provide structure_members with 'width' mapped to Length (correct), 'mass' mapped to Length (wrong type), and no 'name'. Assert result has exactly 2 errors: one TypeMismatch for 'mass' and one MissingParam for 'name'. Should pass immediately — validates multi-requirement handling.

Write test `conformance_let_requirement_checked`: construct a CompiledTrait with a `RequirementKind::Let(Type::Real)` named 'area'. Provide structure_members without 'area'. Assert result contains a MissingLet error. This tests that Let requirements are also validated.

Extend `check_trait_conformance` to also handle `RequirementKind::Let` with the same missing/mismatch logic as Param. Add `MissingLet { name: String, expected_type: Type }` variant to ConformanceError for precision. Update the function to match on both Param and Let requirement kinds.

Write test `conformance_exact_type_equality_dimensions`: construct a trait requiring 'length' with Scalar{LENGTH} dimension, provide structure_members with 'length' mapped to Scalar{MASS}. Assert TypeMismatch. Then test with Scalar{LENGTH} mapped to Scalar{LENGTH} produces no error. Validates that dimensional types use exact equality (no widening).
Run full existing test suite (`cargo test --package reify-compiler`) to verify the rename in pre-1 and all new code don't break any existing tests. This is a regression check.

design_decisions.decision: Create a new public pure function rather than modifying the existing private one
design_decisions.rationale: The existing `check_trait_conformance` does two things: validation AND default injection (value cells, constraints). The task asks for a pure checking function with structured errors. Separating concerns gives callers a clean API for validation-only use cases (e.g., IDE diagnostics, incremental checking) without the side effects of default injection.

Rust doesn't support function overloading, so we can't have two functions with the same name. The existing function both checks conformance AND applies/injects defaults — the new name accurately describes its dual role. Only one call site needs updating.

design_decisions.decision: ConformanceError uses separate MissingParam/MissingLet variants (not a generic Missing variant)
design_decisions.rationale: Structured errors should carry enough information for callers to provide precise diagnostics. Distinguishing param vs let in the error type lets consumers emit different messages or apply different fix suggestions. Follows the existing pattern where RequirementKind distinguishes Param from Let.

design_decisions.decision: Function checks a single CompiledTrait, not the full refinement chain
design_decisions.rationale: The function signature takes `trait_def: &CompiledTrait`, not a trait registry. Hierarchy walking (refinement chains, diamond dedup) remains the caller's responsibility via `collect_all_requirements`. This keeps the function pure and composable — it's infrastructure that higher-level code combines.

design_decisions.decision: Use exact Type equality (PartialEq) for type matching, no widening
design_decisions.rationale: The task explicitly says 'exact type'. The existing conformance code uses `!=` on Type (which derives PartialEq). Int->Real widening is only allowed in expression compilation, not in trait conformance. Dimensional types (Scalar{LENGTH} vs Scalar{MASS}) must match exactly.

design_decisions.decision: Sub requirements are not checked by the new function
design_decisions.rationale: Sub requirements need structure member declarations (SubDecl), not just a name->type map. The function signature `structure_members: &HashMap<String, Type>` can't express sub-component validation. Sub checking remains in the existing `check_and_apply_trait_conformance` function. A future task could add sub checking with an extended input type.

reuse.how: The new function takes &CompiledTrait and iterates its required_members Vec<TraitRequirement>, matching on RequirementKind::Param and ::Let. No new requirement types needed.
Type comparison uses the derived PartialEq — `actual_type != expected_type` for mismatch detection. Covers all Type variants including Scalar with DimensionVector.

reuse.what: Existing test patterns (compile_module, compile_first_template, error filtering)
reuse.how: New tests for the pure function construct CompiledTrait directly (all fields are pub). Integration regression tests reuse existing compile_module helper.

reuse.what: HashMap<String, Type> pattern for structure members
reuse.how: The existing check_and_apply_trait_conformance already builds a HashMap<String, Type> from structure members. The new function accepts this same type as input, so callers can reuse the same collection logic.
Tests use DimensionVector::LENGTH, DimensionVector::MASS etc. to construct Type::Scalar values for test fixtures.
